narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students to predict whether someone is likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and for you, it estimated a 56.6% chance of failing—meaning it saw enough risk to deny your application to protect the school's outcomes. Your profile includes solid strengths like an undergraduate GPA of 3.30, which is above the typical student's 3.21, along with an LSAT score of 34 and coming from an upper-middle-class family background. You're planning to attend full-time, just like most students who do. However, the model's decision was most heavily swayed by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is well below the average of about 5.5 and strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure. This was the top factor pulling against you. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest favorable influence, actively working to lower the failure risk more than any other feature. Your family income level nudged things slightly toward higher risk, while your LSAT score helped pull in the direction of passing, though not as powerfully as your GPA. Your Year 1 decile of 4, a bit below average, added a small push toward failure risk, and your full-time status offered minor support toward success. Compared to typical students, your GPA stands out as a real asset above the norm, but your later school deciles lag behind the pack, which tipped the scales. While this stings, especially as a Black woman navigating systemic hurdles, it's not the end—consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to the average 36.7, strengthening your Year 3 performance through targeted study, or exploring programs with holistic reviews that value your unique background. You have the grit to improve these areas and reapply stronger; many do. Keep pushing forward.
